Keywords: issue, question, why, what, exploration, open-ended, diagnostic
Description: Breaks a problem into sub-questions. Each branch asks 'what are the possible reasons/factors?' Explores all possible issues without presuming answers.
When to Use: When you dont have a strong initial hypothesis; When exploring root causes; When the problem is new or unfamiliar; Early-stage problem structuring
Structure Pattern: Root Question > Sub-Question 1 (Why/What factor?) > Sub-Sub-Question 1a > ... ; Root Question > Sub-Question 2 > ...
Example Application: Why is revenue declining? > Is it a volume problem? (Units sold dropping?) > Is it a price problem? (Price per unit falling?) > Is it a mix problem? (Shifting to lower-margin products?)
MECE Test: Each sub-question must be independent (ME); Together they must cover all possible answers (CE); No branch should overlap with another; All branches together should explain the parent
Common Mistakes: Making branches that overlap; Stopping too early; Mixing levels of abstraction; Including solutions in the tree

Keywords: hypothesis, answer-first, testing, assertion, deductive, speed
Description: Starts with a proposed answer and breaks it into conditions that must be true. Each branch states an assertion to be tested rather than a question to explore.
When to Use: When you have domain expertise; When time is limited; When you need to move fast; When the problem domain is well-understood; When you need a Day 1 answer
Structure Pattern: Hypothesis (Our answer is X) > Must be true: Condition A > Evidence needed for A ; > Must be true: Condition B > Evidence needed for B
Example Application: We should enter Market X > Market is large enough (>$1B TAM) > We can win share (>5% in 3 years) > Returns exceed hurdle rate (>15% IRR) > We have required capabilities
MECE Test: Each condition must be independent; Conditions together must be sufficient to prove/disprove the hypothesis; Failing any one condition should challenge the hypothesis
Common Mistakes: Stacking the deck (only favorable conditions); Missing critical conditions; Not being willing to kill the hypothesis; Conditions that are too easy to satisfy

Keywords: profit, revenue, cost, margin, financial, business, P&L
Description: Classic decomposition of business performance into revenue and cost components. Revenue = Price x Volume. Costs = Fixed + Variable.
When to Use: Any business performance question; Profitability analysis; Margin improvement; Business case evaluation; P&L analysis
Structure Pattern: Profit > Revenue (Price x Volume) > Revenue per unit > Number of units ; Profit > Costs > Fixed costs (rent, salaries, depreciation) > Variable costs (materials, commissions, shipping)
Example Application: Company profit fell 20% > Revenue down 10% (volume flat but price -10%) > Costs up 12% (raw materials +25%, labor +5%, fixed costs flat) > Conclusion: pricing pressure + input cost inflation
MECE Test: Revenue and cost branches are mutually exclusive; Price and volume explain revenue fully; Fixed and variable explain costs fully
Common Mistakes: Mixing revenue and cost items; Ignoring cross-effects (price affects volume); Not going deep enough on the biggest driver; Treating all costs as one bucket

Keywords: customer, funnel, conversion, journey, awareness, purchase, retention, lifecycle
Description: Breaks down customer-related problems along the customer lifecycle: Awareness > Consideration > Purchase > Experience > Loyalty/Advocacy.
When to Use: Customer acquisition problems; Conversion optimization; Customer experience issues; Retention analysis; Marketing effectiveness
Structure Pattern: Problem > Awareness stage (Do they know about us?) > Consideration (Do they evaluate us?) > Purchase (Do they buy?) > Experience (Are they satisfied?) > Retention (Do they stay/refer?)
Example Application: Why is customer acquisition cost rising? > Awareness (reach is declining, CPM up 30%) > Consideration (click-through rate flat) > Conversion (landing page conversion down 15%) > Focus: fix awareness costs and landing page
MECE Test: Each stage is distinct; A customer must pass through earlier stages to reach later ones; Together they cover the full lifecycle
Common Mistakes: Treating the funnel as linear only; Ignoring loops (referrals, repeat purchase); Not measuring stage-to-stage conversion; Focusing on top of funnel when leak is at bottom

Keywords: process, steps, workflow, bottleneck, throughput, lean, operations, efficiency
Description: Decomposes a problem along process steps. Identifies where in the process the breakdown occurs. Good for operational and efficiency problems.
When to Use: Process improvement; Bottleneck identification; Quality problems; Cycle time reduction; Operational efficiency; Supply chain issues
Structure Pattern: Problem > Step 1: Input/Sourcing > Step 2: Processing > Step 3: Quality Check > Step 4: Output/Delivery > For each: Time, Quality, Cost analysis
Example Application: Why are delivery times increasing? > Sourcing (supplier lead times up 5 days) > Manufacturing (unchanged) > Quality check (failure rate up causing rework) > Shipping (carrier delays in region X)
MECE Test: Each process step is distinct and sequential; Together they cover the entire process; Time/cost can be attributed to each step without overlap
Common Mistakes: Not mapping the actual process (mapping the ideal); Ignoring parallel processes; Missing feedback loops; Not measuring at each step; Focusing on steps that feel wrong rather than measuring all

Keywords: segment, customer-type, demographic, behavioral, needs-based, market, targeting
Description: Breaks a market or customer base into distinct segments. Can segment by demographics, behavior, needs, geography, or value.
When to Use: Market sizing; Customer strategy; Product-market fit; Pricing strategy; Go-to-market planning; Portfolio analysis
Structure Pattern: Market > Segment A (by need/behavior) > Size, Growth, Our share ; Market > Segment B > ... ; Validate: segments are MECE and each is internally homogeneous
Example Application: Which customer segments should we prioritize? > Enterprise ($10M+ revenue, 500 companies, 30% margins) > Mid-market ($1-10M, 5000 companies, 20% margins) > SMB (<$1M, 50000 companies, 10% margins)
MECE Test: Segments dont overlap; Together they cover the full market; Each segment has distinct characteristics and needs; Segmentation is actionable
Common Mistakes: Over-segmenting (too many small segments); Segmenting on demographics when behavior differs; Not validating segments with data; Creating segments you cant reach differently

Keywords: bottom-up, grouping, pattern, theme, insight, inductive, clustering
Description: Start with individual findings/data points and group them into themes. Used in synthesis phase to organize diverse evidence into a coherent structure. Also used as a bottom-up discovery tool to build trees from specific cases when general principles are unknown.
When to Use: Synthesizing research findings; Organizing interview insights; Making sense of diverse data; Post-analysis integration; When deductive structure doesnt fit the evidence; Exploring new problem spaces; Building decision frameworks from cases
Structure Pattern: Observation 1 + Observation 2 > Theme A (with 'so what') ; Observation 3 + Observation 4 > Theme B (with 'so what') ; Theme A + Theme B > Governing insight
Example Application: After 20 customer interviews: Group findings into (1) Users want speed over features (2) Trust is the main barrier (3) Pricing is secondary > Governing insight: Focus on fast, trustworthy MVP
MECE Test: Each theme captures distinct insights; No observation is double-counted; Themes together tell the full story; Each theme has a clear 'so what'
Common Mistakes: Groups that overlap; Missing outlier observations; Themes that are too broad; No 'so what' for each theme; Not testing alternative groupings

Keywords: system, feedback, loop, causal, interconnected, dynamic, emergent, complexity
Description: Maps causal relationships and feedback loops in complex systems. Shows how variables influence each other, including reinforcing and balancing loops.
When to Use: Wicked problems; Policy analysis; Ecosystem dynamics; Understanding unintended consequences; Complex organizational issues; Market dynamics
Structure Pattern: Variable A --[+]--> Variable B --[+]--> Variable C --[-]--> Variable A (balancing loop) ; Identify: reinforcing loops (vicious/virtuous cycles), balancing loops (stabilizers), delays
Example Application: Why does employee turnover keep increasing? > Low morale > More work per person > Burnout > More turnover > Even lower morale (reinforcing loop) > Intervention point: hire faster to break the loop
MECE Test: All major variables captured; Causal links are directional and signed (+/-); Feedback loops identified; No spurious correlations included as causal
Common Mistakes: Trying to include everything; Missing feedback loops; Confusing correlation with causation; Not identifying intervention points; Making the map too complex to be useful

Keywords: scenario, uncertainty, future, planning, contingency, branching, options
Description: Structures thinking about uncertain futures by identifying key uncertainties and building 2x2 or multi-dimensional scenario grids.
When to Use: High uncertainty situations; Strategic planning; Investment decisions under uncertainty; Policy planning; Risk management; When prediction is unreliable
Structure Pattern: Identify 2 most critical uncertainties > Create 2x2 matrix > Name each quadrant > Develop narrative for each scenario > Identify robust strategies across scenarios
Example Application: Should we invest in Technology X? > Uncertainty 1: Market adoption speed (fast vs slow) > Uncertainty 2: Regulatory environment (favorable vs restrictive) > 4 scenarios with different strategies for each
MECE Test: Uncertainties are truly independent; Scenarios together cover the range of possible futures; Each scenario is plausible; Scenarios are distinct enough to require different strategies
Common Mistakes: Picking certainties instead of uncertainties; Too many dimensions (keep to 2-3); Scenarios that are too similar; Not developing strategies for each; Best case / worst case instead of structural uncertainties

Keywords: component, factor, basic, starting, initial, brainstorm, elements
Description: The simplest starting tree. Breaks a problem into its basic components or factors without requiring testable hypotheses yet.
When to Use: At the very beginning of problem-solving; When you know little about the problem structure; Before building a hypothesis tree
Structure Pattern: Problem > Component 1 > Sub-component 1a ; Problem > Component 2 > Sub-component 2a
Example Application: How to save salmon? > Watershed quality > Ocean conditions > Fishing > Government policy (Later refined into hypotheses)
MECE Test: All major factors are listed without overlap, capturing the whole problem space
Common Mistakes: Mixing factors with hypotheses; Treating it as a final tree instead of a starting point

Framework: Return on Invested Capital (ROIC) / Deductive Tree
Keywords: ROIC, return on capital, deductive, mathematical, financial, profit, business
Description: A mathematically complete tree where branches logically deduct to the parent node. The most powerful tree for business performance problems.
When to Use: Business performance evaluation; Competitive comparison; Capital allocation; Understanding financial drivers
Structure Pattern: ROIC > Return on Sales (Margin) AND Capital Turnover > Revenue & Costs AND Fixed & Working Capital
Example Application: Why is competitor growing faster? > Same ROIC but lower margin compensated by much higher asset productivity (higher inventory turns)
MECE Test: Branches are mathematically linked and exhaustive (e.g., A = B x C)
Common Mistakes: Confusing accounting profit with cash; Not going deep enough into operational drivers of the numbers

Keywords: decision tree, threshold, choices, sequential, logical, criteria
Description: Maps sequential threshold questions and criteria to arrive at specific decisions or actions.
When to Use: When policy or personal decisions depend on specific criteria being met; standardizing repeated complex decisions
Structure Pattern: Node 1 (Question A) > If Yes: Path 1 > If No: Path 2
Example Application: Should we adopt new software? > Does it meet security? (No -> Reject) > Is it cheaper? (Yes -> Accept)
MECE Test: All decision paths are covered; questions have clear mutually exclusive outcomes
Common Mistakes: Questions that are too subjective; Missing edge cases; Overly complex branching

Keywords: supply, demand, economics, policy, resources, pricing, capacity
Description: Cleaves a problem between factors affecting the supply of a resource versus the demand for it.
When to Use: Resource allocation; Infrastructure planning; Pricing strategy; Policy problems (healthcare, energy, water)
Structure Pattern: Problem > Supply (Can we get more? How?) AND Demand (Can we use less? How?)
Example Application: Airport capacity shortage > Supply of slots (build runways, extend hours) vs Demand for slots (congestion pricing, larger planes)
MECE Test: All drivers fall cleanly into affecting either supply or demand
Common Mistakes: Ignoring pricing as a demand lever; Focusing only on supply (building more) instead of demand management

Keywords: principal, agent, incentives, alignment, contract, motivation, organizational
Description: Analyzes problems where an agent (employee/contractor) acts on behalf of a principal (owner/investor), focusing on incentive alignment.
When to Use: Organizational design; Compensation problems; Contracting; Franchise management; Performance issues
Structure Pattern: Problem > Principal Goals (Visibility, Control, Returns) vs Agent Incentives (Effort, Risk, Reward)
Example Application: Why are sales reps discounting too much? > Principal wants profit margin, Agent is commissioned purely on revenue volume.
MECE Test: Captures both sides of the structural relationship and their respective incentives
Common Mistakes: Assuming agents share principal goals without structural incentives; Ignoring asymmetric information

Keywords: collaborate, compete, game theory, strategy, dynamic, rivalry, partnership
Description: Evaluates strategy by differentiating between arenas/players where ruthless competition makes sense versus those requiring collaboration or coopetition.
When to Use: Corporate strategy; Market entry; Platform ecosystems; Joint ventures; IP protection
Structure Pattern: Strategic Goal > Areas to Compete (Zero-sum, differentiation) vs Areas to Collaborate (Growing the pie, standards)
Example Application: Smartphone strategy > Compete fiercely on hardware features but collaborate on standardizing industry charging ports
MECE Test: All strategic options are categorized by relationship posture
Common Mistakes: Treating all market participants purely as competitors; Ignoring multi-play game dynamics (reputation)
